title: Testing React Applications
content:
### Testing React Applications

Testing ensures React components work correctly. The modern testing stack combines Vitest as the test runner and React Testing Library for rendering and interaction.

---

### Testing Pyramid

| Level | Speed | Confidence | Tools |
|-------|-------|------------|-------|
| Unit tests | Fast | Low | Vitest |
| Integration tests | Medium | Medium | Testing Library |
| E2E tests | Slow | High | Playwright, Cypress |

### What to Test

- User interactions (clicks, typing, form submission)
- Conditional rendering (loading, error, empty states)
- Component behavior (state changes, side effects)
- Accessibility (aria attributes, roles)

### What NOT to Test

- Implementation details (internal state, methods)
- Third-party library internals
- Exact CSS classes
- Every line of code (focus on behavior)

### Testing Philosophy

> Test components the way users interact with them. Find elements by accessible roles and text, not by CSS classes or implementation details.

```jsx
// Bad: testing implementation
const button = wrapper.find('.submit-btn');

// Good: testing behavior
const button = screen.getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i });
```

### Test Types

| Test Type | Example |
|-----------|---------|
| Smoke test | Renders without crashing |
| Behavior test | Click, type, verify output |
| Edge case test | Empty list, error state |
| Accessibility test | Labels, roles, aria attributes |

> **Key Point:** Follow the testing trophy, not pyramid. Write more integration tests than unit or E2E tests. Integration tests give the best confidence-to-speed ratio for React apps.
